questions.question: Explain the HttpSession API. How is a session created, used, and destroyed?
keyPoints: Create: request.getSession() creates or retrieves session
Store data: session.setAttribute(name, value)
Retrieve: session.getAttribute(name)
Destroy: session.invalidate() or timeout
JSESSIONID cookie used to identify session

questions.question: Compare and contrast the four session tracking mechanisms in Java Servlets.
keyPoints: Cookies: most common, automatic via JSESSIONID, can be disabled
URL Rewriting: fallback when cookies disabled, session ID in URL
Hidden Form Fields: works only with forms, not hyperlinks
HttpSession API: server-side storage, most powerful and flexible
Trade-offs in security, usability, and browser compatibility

questions.question: Write a servlet-based login application using HttpSession for session management.
keyPoints: Login servlet validates credentials
On success: create session, store user info with setAttribute
Protected pages: check session with getSession(false)
Logout servlet: call session.invalidate()
Configure session timeout in web.xml

questions.question: Explain URL Rewriting as a session tracking mechanism. When and why is it needed?
keyPoints: Used when client has cookies disabled
Session ID appended to URL as query parameter
Use response.encodeURL() and response.encodeRedirectURL()
Drawbacks: URLs become long, session ID visible in bookmarks
Should be used as fallback, not primary mechanism
